content.problemStatement:
Om Khangat has come up with a problem that he thinks can be used in a CodeChef contest, and has submitted his proposal for review.

CodeChef's review panel has NNN judges, each of whom will give Om's problem a point value between 111 and 101010, denoting how good they think it is (111 being the lowest, and 101010 the highest).

A problem is considered good if and only if **every judge** gives it a score that's strictly greater than 444.

You know the point values given by each judge to Om's problem. Can you tell whether his problem is good?


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains one integer NNN — the number of judges.
The next line contains NNN space-separated integers S1,S2,S3,…,SNS_1,S_{2},S_{3},\ldots ,S_{N}S1​,S2​,S3​,…,SN​ — where SiS_iSi​ denotes the score given to Om's problem by the iii-th judge.




Output Format
For each test case, print the answer on a new line: `YES` if Om's problem is good, and `NO` otherwise.


Each character of the output may be printed in either uppercase or lowercase, i.e, the strings `YES`, `yes`, `YeS`, and `yES` will all be treated as equivalent.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 2000
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the number of judges. The second line contains N space‑separated integers S1, S2, …, SN, the scores given by the judges.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if every score is strictly greater than 4, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: scores contain 3 which is ≤4 → NO.\nTest case 2: all scores are >4 → YES.\nTest case 3: last score is 4 (not >4) → NO.
All five scores are strictly greater than 4, so the problem is good.
First test case has a score 4 → NO. Second test case also has a score 4 → NO.

hidden.label: general case with a low score

solution.approach: For each test case read N and then the N scores. Scan the scores; if any score is less than or equal to 4 we can immediately decide the answer is NO. If the scan finishes without finding such a score the answer is YES. This is O(N) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(total N) where total N is the sum of N over all test cases (≤ 2000).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) additional space besides input storage.
